title: Reducer and context boundary

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Reducer and context boundary; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Reducer and context boundary
sources.claim: Reducer plus context can share state and dispatch across a bounded subtree without threading every prop.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Reducer plus context can share state and dispatch across a bounded subtree without threading every prop.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A course editor provides state and dispatch only to its editing subtree and splits stable services from changing data. Your goal is to design and test a provider boundary for a multi-component feature. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: State and events; html forms; useReducer; Component API design. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Reducer plus context can share state and dispatch across a bounded subtree without threading every prop. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: One global context rerenders broad consumers, default values can hide missing providers, and server/client boundaries matter. This draft therefore rejects: Context should replace all props and local state throughout an application.

includes: Share a reducer-backed feature state through a focused provider without making all state global.
Design and test a provider boundary for a multi-component feature.
excludes: One global context rerenders broad consumers, default values can hide missing providers, and server/client boundaries matter.

outcomes: Design and test a provider boundary for a multi-component feature.

examples.title: Reducer and context boundary: concrete case
examples.situation: A course editor provides state and dispatch only to its editing subtree and splits stable services from changing data.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A course editor provides state and dispatch only to its editing subtree and splits stable services from changing data.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use context for genuinely cross-cutting subtree data, keep local state local, and split contexts by change pattern.
examples.result: The result follows because Use context for genuinely cross-cutting subtree data, keep local state local, and split contexts by change pattern.

examples.title: Reducer and context boundary: boundary case
examples.situation: One global context rerenders broad consumers, default values can hide missing providers, and server/client boundaries matter.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: One global context rerenders broad consumers, default values can hide missing providers, and server/client boundaries matter.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Context should replace all props and local state throughout an application.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use context for genuinely cross-cutting subtree data, keep local state local, and split contexts by change pattern.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Context should replace all props and local state throughout an application.
misconceptions.correction: Use context for genuinely cross-cutting subtree data, keep local state local, and split contexts by change pattern.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Context should replace all props and local state throughout an application.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Reducer and context boundary, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Reducer and context boundary before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and test a provider boundary for a multi-component feature. Keep this boundary in view: One global context rerenders broad consumers, default values can hide missing providers, and server/client boundaries matter.

Why is this claim incorrect: Context should replace all props and local state throughout an application.
Apply checkpoint 'keep local state local' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “When an effect is needed”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
